explanation: Consider {tex}x + 2 y \leq 8{/tex} Let x + 2y = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { x } { 8 } + \frac { y } { 4 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} a = 8 , b = 4 Since, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation {tex}x + 2y \leqslant 8{/tex} Therefore, its solution contains (0, 0) Again {tex}3x + 2y \leqslant 12{/tex} Let 3x + 2y = 12 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{4} + \frac{y}{6} = 1{/tex} Again, (0, 0) satisfies {tex}3x + 2y \leqslant 2{/tex} Therefore its solution contains (0, 0). The feasible region is the solution set which is double shaded and is OABCO. At O (0, 0) Z = 0 At A (4, 0) Z = –3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = –12 At B (2, 3) Z = –3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 6 At C (0, 4) Z = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 16 Hence, minimum Z = –12 at x = 4, y = 0.

We have linear constraints as

Equations Point of Intersection

  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(0, 50){/tex}
  when {tex}y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = 100{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point {tex}(100, 0){/tex}
(i) and (iii) {tex}x = 50, y = 100{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(50, 100){/tex}
(i) and (iv) {tex}x = 0 , y = 0{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(0, 0){/tex}
(i) and (iv) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 200
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is (0, 200)
  when {tex}y = 0 , x = 100{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(100, 0){/tex}

Now for feasible region, using origin testing method for each constraint
For {tex}x + 2 y \geq 100{/tex}, let {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 \geq 100{/tex} i.e., true {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the shaded region will be away from the origin
For {tex}2 x + y \leq 200{/tex}, let {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 \leq 200{/tex} i.e., true {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The shaded region will be toward the origin.
Also, non-negative restrictions {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex} indicates that the feasible region will be exist in first quadrant.
Now, corner points are {tex}A(0, 50), B(20, 40), C(50, 100)\ and\ D(0, 200){/tex}

For optimal solution substituting the value of all comer points in {tex}Z = x + 2y{/tex}

Corner points {tex}Z = x + 2y{/tex}

question_text: Show that the minimum of Z occurs at more than two points. Maximize Z = -x + 2y subject to the constraints: {tex}x \geq 3,x + y \geq 5,x + 2y \geq 6,y \geq 0{/tex} .
explanation: Consider {tex}x \geqslant 3{/tex} Let x = 3 which is a line parallel to y-axis at a positive distance of 3 from it. Since {tex}x \geqslant 3{/tex} , therefore the required half-plane does not contain (0, 0). Now consider {tex}x + y \geqslant 5{/tex} Let x + y = 5 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{5} + \frac{y}{5} = 1{/tex} Now (0, 0) does not satisfy {tex}x + y \geqslant 5{/tex} , therefore the required half plane does not contain (0, 0). Again consider {tex}x + 2y \geqslant 6{/tex} . Let x + 2y = 6 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{6} + \frac{y}{3} = 1{/tex} Here also (0, 0) does not satisfy {tex}x + 2y \geqslant6{/tex} , therefore the required half plane does not contain (0, 0). The corners of the feasible region are A (6, 0), B (4, 1) and C (3, 2). At A (6, 0) Z = –6 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 = –6 At B (4, 1) Z = –4 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 1 = –2 At C (3, 2) Z = –3 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 2 = 1 Hence, maximum Z = 1 at x = 3, y = 2.

Again consider {tex}x + 2y \geqslant 6{/tex} . Let x + 2y = 6 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{6} + \frac{y}{3} = 1{/tex} Here also (0, 0) does not satisfy {tex}x + 2y \geqslant6{/tex} , therefore the required half plane does not contain (0, 0). The corners of the feasible region are A (6, 0), B (4, 1) and C (3, 2). At A (6, 0) Z = –6 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 = –6 At B (4, 1) Z = –4 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 1 = –2 At C (3, 2) Z = –3 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 2 = 1 Hence, maximum Z = 1 at x = 3, y = 2.

question_text: Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically: Maximise Z = 3x + 4y subject to the constraints: {tex}x + y \le4, \ x \geq 0, \ y \geq 0{/tex}
explanation: As {tex}x \geqslant 0,y \geqslant 0{/tex} , therefore we shall shade the other inequalities in the first quadrant only. Now {tex}x + y \leqslant 4{/tex} Let x + y = 4 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{4} + \frac{y}{4} = 1{/tex} Thus the line has 4 and 4 as intercepts along the axes. Now, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation, i.e., {tex}0 + 0 \leqslant 4{/tex} . Therefore, shaded region OAB is the feasible solution. Its corners are O (0, 0), A (4, 0), B (0, 4) At O (0, 0) Z = 0 At A (4, 0) Z = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 12 At B (0, 4) Z = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 16 Hence, max Z = 16 at x = 0, y = 4.

solution_html:
As {tex}x \geqslant 0,y \geqslant 0{/tex}, therefore we shall shade the other inequalities in the first quadrant only.
Now {tex}x + y \leqslant 4{/tex} 

Let x + y = 4
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{4} + \frac{y}{4} = 1{/tex}
Thus the line has 4 and 4 as intercepts along the axes. Now, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation, i.e., {tex}0 + 0 \leqslant 4{/tex}. Therefore, shaded region OAB is the feasible solution.
Its corners are O (0, 0), A (4, 0), B (0, 4)
At O (0, 0) Z = 0
At A (4, 0) Z = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 12
At B (0, 4) Z = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 16
Hence, max Z = 16 at x = 0, y = 4.
